title: What is a Variable? 📦
description: Learn how to store data in the computer's memory.

slides.title: The Memory Box 📦
slides.content:
Imagine the computer's memory is a massive warehouse filled with millions of empty boxes.

A **Variable** is simply giving one of those boxes a name and putting a value inside it. You can then use the name to look inside the box later.

slides.title: Declaring & Assigning
slides.content: In C, you must **declare** the type of data the box will hold *before* you use it. C is strictly typed!
slides.codeSnippet:
int age;    // Declaration (reserving the box)
age = 25;   // Assignment (putting a value in the box)

int score = 100; // Doing both at once (Initialization)

quizData.question: What does a variable declaration conceptually do in C?

options.text: It prints the variable to the screen.
It reserves a labeled box in memory for a specific type of data.

quizData.explanation: Declaring a variable tells the compiler to reserve a spot in memory of a specific size with your chosen name.

slides.title: Naming Rules 📜
slides.content:
You can't name your variables just anything. C has strict rules:

1. Name must start with a **letter** or an **underscore** (`_`).
2. It CANNOT start with a number.
3. It can only contain alpha-numeric characters and underscores.
4. Variable names are **case-sensitive** (`Age` is different from `age`).

quizData.question: Which of the following is an INVALID variable name in C?
quizData.explanation: Variable names can never start with a number.

slides.title: Printing Variables
slides.content: To print a variable using `printf`, you need a **format specifier** (like `%d` for integer) to show *where* the variable goes in the text.
